medications.frequency: as needed, up to 4 times daily

family_history: Mother: rheumatoid arthritis, died age 78 of myocardial infarction
Father: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, heavy smoker, died age 71
No family history of epilepsy, dementia, or psychiatric illness

social_history.tobacco: Former heavy smoker, 50 pack-year history, quit 2 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, approximately 1-2 per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a detached house; three adult children living nearby
social_history.exercise: Light walking, limited by knee pain

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but disoriented. Oriented to person only; unable to state the date, month, year, or hospital name. Speech is fluent but with intermittent word-finding difficulty and occasional paraphasic errors. She becomes easily frustrated when unable to answer questions and at times becomes tearful without clear provocation. Attention is impaired; unable to perform serial 7s or spell WORLD backward. Short-term memory is profoundly affected; recalls 0 of 3 objects at 5 minutes. Abbreviated mental test score 7/10. She intermittently stares blankly for several seconds before resuming interaction.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements are full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. No facial asymmetry. Hearing is grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue is midline without fasciculations. Subtle intermittent lip-pursing movements noted during examination.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone is normal in all extremities. Strength is 5/5 throughout when patient cooperates, though effort is intermittently inconsistent. No pronator drift. No muscle wasting. Occasional involuntary jerking movements of the right hand noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick in all extremities. Proprioception is intact at the great toes bilaterally. Detailed sensory examination limited by patient's fluctuating attention.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk), Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing shows mild bilateral dysmetria, though this is likely confounded by inattention. Heel-to-shin testing is grossly normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly unsteady with slight widening of base. Able to walk without assistance but requires supervision. Tandem gait is impaired.
neurological_exam.additional: Significant postural hypotension noted: blood pressure drops from 107/75 mmHg supine to 82/58 mmHg upon standing. Intermittent orofacial dyskinesias observed, including lip-pursing and subtle tongue protrusion movements. One brief episode of behavioral arrest lasting approximately 15 seconds witnessed during the examination, with staring and rhythmic lip movements, followed by brief confusion. Heart rate varies between 56 and 78 bpm during the encounter.

patient.chief_complaint: Collapse with brief loss of consciousness; progressive memory decline over three months
patient.history_present_illness: This 62-year-old right-handed retired school administrator presents to the emergency department following a witnessed collapse at home with brief loss of consciousness lasting approximately 30 seconds. Her husband reports that she was standing in the kitchen when she suddenly became unresponsive and fell to the ground. There was no witnessed tonic-clonic activity, tongue biting, or urinary incontinence, though she was briefly confused upon regaining consciousness. She has no recollection of the event. More significantly, her family has become increasingly concerned about progressive deterioration in her short-term memory over the preceding three months. Initially, she began misplacing household items and forgetting recent conversations, which both she and her husband attributed to stress related to her recent retirement. Over the following weeks, she forgot appointments, repeated questions she had asked minutes earlier, and on one occasion left the front door wide open overnight. She has also exhibited uncharacteristic irritability, emotional lability, and intermittent episodes where she appears to 'zone out' for several seconds before resuming activity. Her husband reports at least three such episodes in the past two weeks, during which she stares blankly and makes subtle repetitive mouth movements. She has also complained of persistent insomnia over the past month despite no change in her citalopram dose. Over the most recent week, her confusion has worsened noticeably: she became lost on a familiar walk to the local shop, could not recall her grandchildren's names, and was found attempting to use the television remote to make a phone call. She has had no recent illness, fever, headache, or focal weakness. There is no history of recent travel, tick exposure, or new sexual contacts. She has no prior psychiatric hospitalization or psychotic episodes. Her extensive smoking history is noted but she quit two years ago after a health scare involving a persistent cough that resolved with cessation. She continues to use her inhalers as prescribed for mild residual symptoms.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly organized 7 Hz theta activity replacing expected alpha rhythm over occipital regions

findings.frequency: 4-6 Hz theta and intermittent delta range activity
findings.clinical_correlation: Indicates diffuse cerebral dysfunction; differential includes encephalitis, metabolic encephalopathy, or neurodegenerative process

findings.morphology: Notched delta waves, moderate amplitude (80-120 microvolts)
findings.clinical_correlation: Frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity; non-specific, may be seen in encephalopathies of various etiologies including metabolic, toxic, and inflammatory causes

findings.morphology: Rhythmic 5 Hz theta activity evolving to sharply contoured waves before offset
findings.state: Correlated with observed behavioral arrest and lip automatisms
findings.clinical_correlation: Suggestive of focal seizure of temporal origin; however, could also represent an artifact or a non-epileptiform pattern. Clinical correlation required.

artifacts.description: Intermittent temporal muscle artifact, particularly during periods of agitation
Brief epochs of movement artifact associated with orofacial dyskinesias

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed; patient unable to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating moderate diffuse background slowing, frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (FIRDA), and one suspicious electrographic event from the left temporal region correlating with observed behavioral arrest. The overall pattern is consistent with diffuse cerebral dysfunction with possible focal epileptogenicity. The differential includes encephalitic process, metabolic encephalopathy, or early neurodegenerative disease. No extreme delta brush pattern is identified. Continuous EEG monitoring is recommended.
eeg.limitations: 30-minute routine recording with intermittent artifact; prolonged monitoring recommended for more comprehensive evaluation
recommended_actions: Continuous EEG monitoring for 24-48 hours to assess seizure burden
Metabolic workup to exclude reversible causes of encephalopathy
Consider lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
Brain MRI if not already obtained

findings.location: Left medial temporal lobe, predominantly involving the hippocampus

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity, could represent early pathology versus artifact

findings.location: Periventricular and subcortical white matter, bilateral

additional_observations: Ventricles are mildly prominent, compatible with age-related involutional changes
Mild generalized cerebral atrophy appropriate for age
mri.impression: Subtle FLAIR hyperintensity in the left medial temporal lobe without restricted diffusion, enhancement, or mass effect. This finding is equivocal and may represent early inflammatory pathology versus artifact. The finding could be consistent with limbic encephalitis but is not specific. Mild scattered periventricular white matter changes and generalized cerebral atrophy are likely age-related. No evidence of metastatic disease or acute infarction. Clinical correlation is strongly recommended.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Medial temporal FLAIR signal is consistent with limbic encephalitis, though the finding is subtle and unilateral
Progressive memory loss in an older adult with mild hippocampal signal change; however, AD typically shows hippocampal atrophy rather than FLAIR hyperintensity
Temporal lobe involvement but no hemorrhagic changes, restricted diffusion, or enhancement typical of HSV
The finding is subtle enough that artifact cannot be excluded; follow-up imaging may clarify

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with CSF analysis and serological testing
Consider autoimmune and paraneoplastic antibody panels
Repeat MRI in 7-14 days if clinical suspicion persists
CT chest if paraneoplastic etiology is considered

ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG. No arrhythmia or conduction abnormality identified that would account for a syncopal episode.
ecg.clinical_correlation: A normal ECG does not exclude paroxysmal arrhythmias. If syncope remains unexplained, consider prolonged cardiac monitoring or tilt-table testing.

BMP.clinical_significance: Hyponatremia; consider SIADH in the setting of CNS pathology, or diuretic effect from co-amilozide

Inflammatory markers.clinical_significance: Upper end of normal for age

Thyroid function.clinical_significance: Normal but upper end of range; does not suggest Hashimoto encephalopathy

labs.interpretation: Hyponatremia (128 mEq/L) which could represent SIADH in the setting of CNS pathology, though the patient's co-amilozide (containing hydrochlorothiazide) is a confounding factor. Mildly elevated CRP is nonspecific. Normal thyroid function argues against Hashimoto encephalopathy. Normal CK, hepatic function, and renal function. CBC is unremarkable. The hyponatremia warrants further workup with serum and urine osmolality studies.
abnormal_values_summary: Sodium: 128 mEq/L - Hyponatremia; consider SIADH vs. diuretic effect
Chloride: 94 mEq/L - Low, consistent with hyponatremia
Glucose: 102 mg/dL - Mildly elevated, likely stress response

special_tests.Cytology: Rare reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells
special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Pending (sent to reference laboratory, expected turnaround 5-7 business days)

csf.interpretation: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (8 cells/uL, 85% lymphocytes) with borderline elevated protein. Glucose and glucose ratio are normal. Oligoclonal bands are equivocal and IgG index is at the upper limit of normal. All viral PCRs are negative, making active viral encephalitis unlikely. Bacterial cultures show no growth and cytology is negative for malignancy. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing is pending. The mild CSF inflammatory profile is non-specific and could be seen in early autoimmune encephalitis, low-grade viral process, or even paraneoplastic disease. Clinical correlation and follow-up antibody results are essential.

NMDAR Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Positive in serum; CSF testing is more sensitive and specific, particularly in older patients
CSF titer exceeds serum titer, confirming intrathecal antibody production. Diagnostic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Titers are relatively modest, possibly reflecting the older age of onset.

output.interpretation: Both serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive. The CSF titer (1:64) exceeds the serum titer (1:40), confirming intrathecal antibody production and establishing the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. The relatively modest titers may reflect the patient's older age of onset, which is associated with lower antibody levels and less frequent teratoma association. First-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone and IVIG or plasma exchange should be initiated promptly.
abnormal_values_summary: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG: Positive (titer 1:40) - Positive in serum
CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG: Positive (titer 1:64) - Confirms intrathecal antibody production, diagnostic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Atrophic, 1.5 x 1.0 x 0.8 cm, no discrete lesions
Atrophic, 1.3 x 0.9 x 0.7 cm, no discrete lesions
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Normal postmenopausal ovary; no teratoma identified

output.interpretation: Normal postmenopausal pelvic ultrasound. Both ovaries are atrophic without discrete masses or teratoma. The absence of ovarian teratoma is expected in this age group; anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in patients over 45 years is less commonly associated with ovarian teratoma. A comprehensive paraneoplastic workup with CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis should be considered to evaluate for other underlying malignancies.

Autoimmune Encephalitis Panel.clinical_significance: Negative paraneoplastic antibody; does not exclude paraneoplastic etiology entirely

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autoimmune and paraneoplastic encephalitis panel confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibody positivity in CSF (1:64). All other neuronal surface antibodies, classical paraneoplastic antibodies (Hu, CV2/CRMP5), and ANA are negative. Anti-TPO is normal, ruling out Hashimoto encephalopathy. Given the patient's age and smoking history, CT of the chest is recommended to evaluate for occult malignancy even though classical paraneoplastic antibodies are negative, as anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in older patients can rarely be paraneoplastic.
abnormal_values_summary: Anti-NMDA receptor Ab (CSF): Positive (1:64) - Diagnostic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

findings.mass_effect: Encasing the left lower lobe pulmonary artery

signal_characteristics.density: Soft tissue density with irregular margins

additional_observations: Bilateral ovaries are atrophic without masses
No skeletal lesions suspicious for metastatic disease
Mild emphysematous changes in bilateral upper lobes consistent with smoking history
output.impression: Left lower lobe pulmonary parenchymal opacity with associated left hilar lymphadenopathy encasing the left lower lobe pulmonary artery. The appearance is highly suspicious for primary bronchogenic carcinoma, particularly given the patient's significant smoking history. Mild emphysematous changes are noted. No evidence of distant metastatic disease on this study. Tissue diagnosis is recommended. This finding raises the possibility of a paraneoplastic etiology for the patient's neurological presentation.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Bronchogenic carcinoma (small cell or non-small cell)
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Spiculated left lower lobe mass with hilar lymphadenopathy in a heavy former smoker is highly suspicious for primary lung malignancy
Hilar lymphadenopathy could represent lymphoma but parenchymal mass is less typical
Hilar adenopathy can be seen but the spiculated parenchymal mass is atypical

recommended_actions: CT-guided biopsy or bronchoscopy with biopsy for tissue diagnosis
PET-CT for staging if malignancy confirmed

cell_count.WBC: 18 cells/uL (elevated, increased from 8 on prior LP)

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (3 bands, not present in matched serum; increased from equivocal on prior LP)

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture shows worsening lymphocytic pleocytosis (18 cells/uL, up from 8) and rising protein (58 mg/dL, up from 48). Oligoclonal bands are now definitively positive with 3 bands absent from serum, and IgG index has risen above the normal threshold, indicating progressive intrathecal immune activation. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are confirmed positive. The evolving CSF profile supports active autoimmune encephalitis and the need for aggressive immunotherapy.

output.query: paraneoplastic and autoimmune encephalitis in older adults NMDAR antibodies

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in older adults: clinical features and outcomes
results.key_finding: Patients over 45 years represent approximately 5% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases. They are less likely to have ovarian teratoma but more likely to have other underlying neoplasms. Clinical presentation may include more prominent cognitive features and less frequent psychiatric symptoms compared to younger patients.

results.title: Clinical features of anti-NMDAR encephalitis associated with lung cancer
results.key_finding: Small cell lung carcinoma has been reported as a rare trigger for anti-NMDAR encephalitis, particularly in older patients with smoking history. The paraneoplastic association should be considered in any older patient with anti-NMDAR encephalitis and risk factors for lung cancer.

results.title: Distinguishing paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis from anti-NMDAR encephalitis
results.key_finding: Classical paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis (anti-Hu, anti-CV2) and anti-NMDAR encephalitis can overlap clinically. Key distinguishing features: NMDAR encephalitis typically shows prominent psychiatric features, dyskinesias, and autonomic instability, while classical paraneoplastic LE shows more isolated memory deficits. Both can be associated with underlying malignancy.

output.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is uncommon in patients over 45 but well-documented. In older patients, ovarian teratoma is rare as an underlying cause; instead, other neoplasms including small cell lung carcinoma should be investigated. The clinical presentation may emphasize cognitive decline over psychiatric symptoms. Both first-line immunotherapy (steroids, IVIG, or plasmapheresis) and treatment of any underlying malignancy are critical for optimal outcomes.

output.proposed: IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 5 days, followed by IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days
interactions: Methylprednisolone + hydrochlorothiazide (co-amilozide): May worsen hypokalemia and hyponatremia; monitor electrolytes closely
Methylprednisolone may elevate blood glucose; monitor for hyperglycemia
IVIG can cause fluid overload; monitor in setting of potential cardiac comorbidity
warnings: Patient is on citalopram; high-dose steroids may cause psychiatric side effects (insomnia, agitation, psychosis) that can be difficult to distinguish from the underlying encephalitis
Consider holding co-amilozide during high-dose steroid therapy due to electrolyte effects and reassess hyponatremia etiology
Penicillin allergy noted; not relevant to proposed immunotherapy but important for any concurrent antibiotic considerations
output.formulary_status: IV methylprednisolone and IVIG both available on formulary
alternatives: Plasma exchange (PLEX) 5 sessions as alternative to or in combination with IVIG for first-line immunotherapy
If insufficient response: rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 as second-line therapy

Osmolality Studies.clinical_significance: Inappropriately concentrated relative to low serum osmolality, consistent with SIADH rather than diuretic-induced hyponatremia alone
Elevated urine sodium with hyponatremia supports SIADH over volume depletion

output.interpretation: Hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine and elevated urine sodium is most consistent with SIADH, likely secondary to CNS inflammation. While the patient's co-amilozide could contribute to hyponatremia, the pattern of inappropriately concentrated urine with high urine sodium points toward SIADH as the primary driver. Recommend holding co-amilozide, implementing fluid restriction, and monitoring sodium every 6-8 hours. Avoid rapid overcorrection to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.
abnormal_values_summary: Serum osmolality: 262 mOsm/kg - Low, consistent with hypotonic hyponatremia
Urine sodium: 42 mEq/L - Elevated, supports SIADH

differential.likelihood: Moderate initially, low after antibody panel
differential.key_distinguishing: The patient's heavy smoking history and subsequent discovery of a suspicious lung mass raises strong concern for classical paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis. However, anti-Hu and anti-CV2 antibodies are negative, and the presence of orofacial dyskinesias, behavioral arrest episodes, and autonomic instability are more characteristic of anti-NMDAR encephalitis than classical LE. Anti-NMDAR antibody positivity confirms the diagnosis, though the lung lesion may still represent the immunological trigger.

differential.diagnosis: Early Alzheimer disease or rapidly progressive dementia
differential.key_distinguishing: Progressive memory loss over 3 months in a 62-year-old could initially suggest a neurodegenerative process. However, the subacute tempo, presence of orofacial dyskinesias, behavioral arrest episodes, autonomic instability, and CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis argue against a purely neurodegenerative process. AD does not typically cause CSF pleocytosis or dyskinesias.

Temporal lobe involvement on MRI overlaps with HSV encephalitis, but the subacute 3-month course is far more indolent than typical HSV encephalitis. All viral PCRs in CSF are negative, and MRI lacks hemorrhagic changes or restricted diffusion typical of HSV.

The lung mass raises concern for cerebral metastases causing cognitive decline. However, brain MRI shows no enhancing lesions or mass lesions consistent with metastatic disease. The medial temporal FLAIR signal is not consistent with metastatic morphology.

Family history of Hashimoto thyroiditis in the sister and upper-normal TSH could raise this consideration. However, anti-TPO antibodies are normal and thyroid function is intact. Hashimoto encephalopathy requires markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: ensure IV access, continuous cardiac and pulse oximetry monitoring, seizure precautions. Evaluate for reversible causes of syncope and confusion.
Obtain comprehensive labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, ESR, hepatic panel, thyroid function, CK, urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia (128 mEq/L), mildly elevated CRP, normal thyroid function, otherwise unremarkable

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle left medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity, equivocal but warranting correlation; no brain metastases

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF sent for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, bacterial culture, viral PCRs (HSV, VZV, enterovirus), oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, borderline protein, equivocal oligoclonal bands, negative viral PCRs, NMDAR antibodies pending

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG; if abnormal, initiate continuous EEG monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse slowing, FIRDA, possible electrographic seizure of temporal origin

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage until HSV is excluded

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 500-1000 mg IV q12h) given clinical seizure activity
Order serum and urine osmolality studies to distinguish SIADH from diuretic-induced hyponatremia; consider holding co-amilozide
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Findings consistent with SIADH rather than diuretic effect alone

optimal_actions.action: Given clinical suspicion for autoimmune encephalitis (dyskinesias, behavioral arrest, autonomic instability, subacute course), initiate empiric first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Early treatment initiation improves outcomes; do not wait for antibody confirmation when clinical suspicion is high

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis for malignancy screening given age, smoking history, and possible paraneoplastic etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left lower lobe lung mass with hilar lymphadenopathy suspicious for bronchogenic carcinoma

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal postmenopausal ovaries; no teratoma (expected given age)

optimal_actions.action: Add IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days once NMDAR antibodies confirmed or if clinical deterioration continues
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Combined immunotherapy with steroids and IVIG

optimal_actions.action: Refer for CT-guided lung biopsy and oncology consultation for the lung mass
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Tissue diagnosis of the lung lesion; treatment of underlying malignancy if confirmed as paraneoplastic trigger

optimal_actions.action: Implement fluid restriction for SIADH-associated hyponatremia with serial sodium monitoring every 6-8 hours
If insufficient clinical improvement after 2 weeks of first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improved neurological function with B-cell depletion

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Recognize that orofacial dyskinesias, behavioral arrest episodes, and autonomic instability in the setting of subacute cognitive decline are red flags for autoimmune encephalitis
Initiate immunotherapy empirically based on clinical suspicion rather than delaying for antibody results
Screen for underlying malignancy with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis given age and smoking history
Evaluate hyponatremia etiology (SIADH vs. diuretic-induced) with osmolality studies
Initiate anti-seizure medication given witnessed clinical seizure activity
Admit to a monitored setting given seizures, autonomic instability, and orthostatic hypotension
contraindicated_actions: Attributing the cognitive decline solely to early Alzheimer disease or normal aging and deferring further workup
Administering high-dose antipsychotics for behavioral disturbance, which can worsen dyskinesias and autonomic instability
Delaying immunotherapy for days while awaiting antibody results from reference laboratory, when clinical suspicion is high
Attributing the syncopal episode solely to orthostatic hypotension from co-amilozide and missing the broader neurological picture
Rapid sodium correction exceeding 8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours, risking osmotic demyelination syndrome
Failing to investigate the lung mass discovered on CT imaging; treating only the encephalitis without addressing a potential paraneoplastic trigger
key_reasoning_points: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis can occur in older adults (>45 years), though it accounts for only approximately 5% of cases; clinicians must not dismiss this diagnosis based on age alone

The source case initially presented as paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis with an underlying small cell lung carcinoma; anti-NMDAR encephalitis can similarly coexist with lung malignancy as a paraneoplastic trigger, particularly in older smokers

Orofacial dyskinesias (lip-pursing, tongue movements) and behavioral arrest episodes are highly suggestive of anti-NMDAR encephalitis and distinguish it from classical paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis or neurodegenerative disease

Hyponatremia in this case has a dual possible etiology: co-amilozide (thiazide diuretic) and SIADH from CNS inflammation. Osmolality studies are needed to differentiate, and the combination makes diagnosis more challenging

The heavy smoking history and subsequent lung mass discovery create a significant diagnostic complexity: the lung lesion could be the paraneoplastic trigger for anti-NMDAR encephalitis, or could suggest classical paraneoplastic LE instead
Subtle or unilateral MRI findings do not exclude limbic encephalitis; the equivocal left temporal FLAIR signal requires clinical correlation and potentially repeat imaging
Mild CSF pleocytosis (WBC 5-20) with pending antibody results demands empiric immunotherapy when clinical features strongly suggest autoimmune encephalitis
Family history of autoimmune disease (sister with Hashimoto thyroiditis, mother with rheumatoid arthritis) increases the pre-test probability for autoimmune conditions
The normal ECG and postural hypotension create a red herring suggesting cardiogenic or vasovagal syncope, potentially distracting from the underlying encephalitis

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to atypical older age of onset (62 years), confounding smoking history and lung mass raising concern for classical paraneoplastic LE versus NMDAR encephalitis, dual etiology for hyponatremia (diuretic vs. SIADH), equivocal MRI findings, mild CSF pleocytosis with pending antibody results, and postural hypotension as a red herring for syncope
